.,nooFFlcEoFctTYo''.o,lJ,="]".,,":.,l,.t\",..o",Calilornia92522.714/782-5567
llay 13, 1991
Fair Political Practice Commission
Legal Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95804-0807
Re: Request for Written Advice
We are writing to request written advice on a
conflict of interest matter in behalf of and on the specific
authorization of Robert A. Buster, a member of the City of
Riverside City Council.
Factual Background
Bob Buster is Councilmember for the Fourth Ward of
the City of Riverside. He and his wife own a home and
citrus ranch of approximately 5 acres in the "Greenbelt"
agricultural area of Riverside. He also owns a one-third
interest in an adjacent 5 acre citrus grove owned by a
family trust in his mother's name. His property is within
the "Greenbelt" area covered by a zoning ordinance which was
adopted by a voter's initiative 
in L979 known as Measure R.
In 1987 voters approved Measure C which among other things
amended Ivleasure R. fhis measure (C ) did not directly af fect
the Buster land. Mr. Buster lives on the property and works
the citrus ranch. He also receives income from the Royal
Citrus Company. This private business operates in the
"Greenbelt" and picks, haulsr packs and markets the fruit
f rom t'lr. Buster 's groves.
The L979 voter initiative, 
l"leasure R, was intended
to reduce urban sprawl, preserve citrus and agricultural
Iandsr pres€rve Riverside's unique hills and arroyos and a
local scenic street, Victoria Avenue. The L9B7 voter
initiative, 
Measure C, amended Measure R by eliminating the
City Council's authority to amend the zoning provisions of
l'leasure R.
In Riverside, approximately 5200 acres are within
the "Greenbelt" i 4500 acres are affected by Measure R and
2300 acres are agricultural land. There were 1530 people
living in the greenbelt area according to the 1980 census.
The City of Riverside covers approximately 49,3O8 acres and
has a 1990 census population of 226,505-
JOHN WOOOHEAD
Clty Attorney


FPPC
May i-3, I99I
Page 2
Riverside is a charter city and operates from a
long-range planning device knottn as the General Plan. The
substance and preparation of general plans are includeil in
Government Code Sections 65350 et seq.
A General PIan does not autl"Iorize development of the
property. 
( See Selby Realty Co. v. City of San Buenaventure
[rs?r] 10 c.:a 1
and development is as follows: l) General Plan; 2) zoning;
3) subdivision or plot plans, 4) building permits. In order
to acquire a right to develop there must be substantial and
detrimental reliance upon an issued building permit. fhe
City of Riverside is now in the process of a General Plan
revision which will be used as the long-range planning
document for 10 or more years. This revision is expected to
be completed by september, 199I . A11 property in the City
will be included in some new or existing land use
designation. Public hearings will be held by the City
Council after hearings before the City's Environmental
Protection Commission and Planning Conmission.
Both Measures c and R are the subject of pending
Iitigation, 
carat v. City of Riverside, Riverside Superior
court Case No. 191567. The local Superior Court in
September, 1989 ruled the City's General PIan invalid, and
as a consequence, Measures C and R were also ruled invalid
because they were allegedly adopted during a time when the
ceneral PIan $ras invaliil. 
The Superior Court's decision has
been appealed and oral argunent is scheduled for June 5,
199I before the Fourth District court of Appeal .
Mr. Buster was active in citizen groups supporting
Measures C and R and their support $ras part of his election
platforn. 
Mr. Buster was first elected to the City Council
in January, 198O in a run-off election following the
Novenber General Election. 
Measure R was passed in the
November, 1979 General Election. Mr. Buster served on the
Council until Llovember, 1983. Mr. Buster was reelected to
the Council in January, I99O and serves presently. Prior to
his reelection to the City Council in 1990 and after 1983,
Mr, Buster served on a citizen committee formed by the City
Council to assist in inplenenting Measure C. He has not
served on that committee since his election to the City


FPPC
May 13, 199I
Page 3
Council in 1990. That committee regularly reviewed the
implementation of Measure C anil reported to the City Council
while Buster lras a member of the committee.
In the coming months matters involving the General
Plan revision will come before the council. As noted the
revision will require a review of prior designations of
property within the city, Mr. Buster's as well as all other
members of the public. 
Matters witl cone before the Council
regarding the implementation of Measures C and R which would
affect his property or his business in the Greenbelt. This
coulil include a aletermination of whe ther a Property is or is
not affected by by the voter measures, or whether the
property is agricultural or hillside ProPerty which would be
prinarily a factual or legal- determination. We recognize
that a decision concerning Mr. Buster's property $rould be
one from which he would be ilisqualified to act and therefore
a matter in which tre could not participate'
Questions
I. 
Does either Mr. Buster's property ownership,
business interest, interest in his family trust in the City
of Riverside cause him to be clisqualified from participating
in any of the following matters?
a. Council ilecisions concerning the appeal of
the Garat case? These questions would concern
themselves with matters of settlenent of the case or
decisions as to further procedure without reference
to the zoning designation of any particular property
-- Mr. Buster's nor anyone else's. 
The ultimate
decision on planning and. Iand uses will be made
through the general plan revision and zoning
ordinance anendments.
b. Is Mr, Buster's interest on the appeal of
the lawsuit the sane as the public generally
considering number of acres, property owners and
residents who will be similarly affected by a
decision on appeal ?


FPPC
May 13, I99I
Page 4
c. Matters before the City Council regarding
the implementation of Measures C and R which affect
property other than his property or his business in
the Greenbelt? As noted, these matters may only
include determinations of whether a property is or
is not affected by the voter approved measures.
il, council ilecisions concerning the General
PIan revi s ions ?
e. If the anshrer to any of the above is that he
cannot participate can the questions be separated?
T'hat is to say can the revisions be presented so as
to allow him to participate on al-I matters except
those concerning his property and only recuse
himself on those items that directly affect his
property. As noted, a General Plan question does
not actually change the zoning or permitted use of
specific property.
The City is very concerned about the challenge to
Mr. Buster as the letter from Mr. Roger Brown (a copy of
which is attacheil) is apparently written on behalf of one of
the litigants against the city in the Garat matter.
strategic decisions concerning the litigation 
are frequently
presented to the Council and this challenge is an obvious
attenpt to silence Plr. Buster's voice on the Council as he
is a known proponent of Measures c & R.
Your prompt
appreciated.
response to these inquiries wiII be
J"*.1. (*rnr
JOHN H. LARSON
Attorney for Robert Buster
uly yourF, ,/
q/*tZ,./
JOHN WOODHEAD
City Attorney
Jw /7 L)LL/ Ln
